content.body: Rectangles and triangles were easy. But what is the area under a genuine curve like f(x) = x²? The trick is to slice the region into many thin strips, treat each as a skinny rectangle, and add them up. The thinner the strips, the better the estimate.
Make the strips infinitely thin and the running total becomes exact. That exact area is the definite integral.
Here is f(x) = x². Drag x to the right and watch the shaded area pile up. As x grows, the area grows…

feedback.correct: Right — because the curve gets taller as x grows, each new strip is taller than the last, so the area piles up faster and faster.
feedback.incorrect: Watch how much area each step adds. As the curve rises, the new strips get taller.
feedback.hint: Taller strips add more area. Since x² keeps rising, the area grows faster and faster.

content.body: Adding up infinitely many strips by hand would be painful, so there's a shortcut. For f(x) = x², the area from 0 to b works out to:
Check it against the graph: at b = 3 this gives 27/3 = 9, which matches the shaded area you swept out.
Use the formula b³/3. What is the area under f(x) = x² from 0 to 2? (Round to two decimals.)
Back to the graph of f(x) = x². Drag x until the shaded area reads about 9. (Remember: the area from 0 to b is b³/3.)

answer.prompt: Drag x until the shaded area reads about 9.

feedback.correct: Nice — at x = 3 the area is 3³/3 = 9. You located exactly where 9 units of area have accumulated.
feedback.incorrect: You need b³/3 = 9, so b³ = 27. Slide until the area readout reaches about 9.

content.body: For f(x) = x², suppose you double the interval from [0, b] to [0, 2b]. The area…

feedback.correct: Right — the area is b³/3, so doubling b gives (2b)³/3 = 8 · b³/3. The area grows eightfold, far more than double.
feedback.incorrect: The area depends on b³, not b. What happens to b³ when you double b?
feedback.hint: (2b)³ = 8b³, so the area becomes 8 times as big — more than double.

content.body: You now know that area under a curve is built from thin strips and captured exactly by the definite integral — and for x² there's a clean formula, b³/3.
But where do formulas like b³/3 come from? The answer ties areas back to derivatives through one of the most important results in all of calculus.
Using the formula b³/3, what is the area under f(x) = x² from 0 to 3?
What is the area under f(x) = x² from 0 to 1? (Round to two decimals.)
To approximate the area under a curve, we add up many thin…

feedback.correct: Correct — we slice the region into thin rectangles (strips) and sum their areas.
feedback.incorrect: Think about the shape of each thin slice under the curve.
feedback.hint: Each thin strip is treated as a skinny rectangle.
